Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I'm juggling a lot at 27 - I have work commitments and other responsibilities that made it hard to stick to a set schedule. I tried to carve out about 15 hours a week, usually in chunks when I could find quiet time. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. I'd set intentions to study more consistently, but life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what was happening in my life outside of school. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, logging in almost daily, but other weeks I'd barely touch the course materials until a deadline loomed. I found myself doing a lot of catch-up studying in bursts rather than maintaining steady progress. The inconsistency frustrated me because I knew better, but I was doing my best with competing demands.
Q3: Usually a deadline approaching would be my main prompt to log in - that sense of urgency got me moving. I also logged in when I received notifications about new content or discussion posts from classmates. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel connected to the course, even if I didn't have specific tasks to complete. The gamified elements actually helped motivate me too - I liked seeing my progress badges and completing challenges.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine alongside checking email. I'd grab my coffee and scroll through any new announcements or discussion activity before starting my day. The discussion forums helped make it feel more social and less like a chore. But honestly, that routine fell apart during particularly stressful periods when I was overwhelmed with other things.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me because I learn best by listening. I watched over two hours of video content and often replayed sections to really absorb the material. The discussion forums were also great - I posted regularly and interacted with peers because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click for me. The gamified tasks made learning feel less like a burden and more engaging.
Q6: I tended to ignore the dense reading materials and text-heavy resources because they just don't work well for how I learn. I'd skim them minimally or try to find audio alternatives online. Some of the supplementary PDFs sat unopened because I was already struggling to keep up with the core content. I knew I should engage with them more, but I had to prioritize what worked for my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually feel myself understanding something new or making connections. The gamified tasks were great because they gave immediate feedback and made progress feel tangible. I also valued activities that let me interact with other students because learning feels more meaningful when it's social. If something felt like busywork without clear learning outcomes, I'd lose motivation quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford given my time constraints. Required activities got priority because they directly impacted my grade, while optional ones often got pushed aside. That said, when I did engage with optional content, it felt more enjoyable because there was no pressure. I wish I'd had more time for the optional discussion threads because those conversations were actually really enriching.
Q9: Absolutely - I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen while walking or driving, which was a huge part of my learning process. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos to supplement topics I was struggling with. My notes from listening sessions were extensive, but none of that showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deeply engaged with course material but completely offline. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times, pausing to take notes and really process the information. Sometimes I'd spend an hour discussing concepts with a friend who's in a similar field, working through problems together. My best learning often happened away from the computer, in moments of reflection and conversation.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my primary mode of studying. I'd access a video lecture once, download it or take detailed notes, and then return to those materials repeatedly offline. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have engaged with that content five or six more times. It was more convenient and fit better with my lifestyle to study on the go.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester when my stress levels peaked and everything felt overwhelming. Work got really demanding, and I was dealing with some personal stuff that drained my energy. My attendance dropped significantly during that time, and I fell behind on assignments. Even though I have high resilience, those weeks were really tough and I struggled to engage meaningfully with anything.
Q13: Definitely - when I was exhausted from work or stressed about other responsibilities, the LMS became just another source of anxiety. I'd log in with good intentions but find myself unable to focus or retain information. Sometimes I'd open materials and just stare at them, too fatigued to actually process anything. The competing demands of being a 27-year-old with multiple responsibilities made consistent engagement really challenging.
Q14: Yes, there were definitely times I'd watch videos or scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd be physically present but mentally elsewhere, thinking about work deadlines or personal issues. Sometimes I'd complete the gamified tasks just for the points without really engaging with the underlying concepts. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments repeatedly, even knowing they were crucial for my grade. Part of it was anxiety - the larger the task, the more overwhelming it felt to start. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, which became an excuse to delay. Looking back, this avoidance pattern really hurt my assignment completion rate and ultimately my grade.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main engine for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, cramming in video watching and discussion posts. This created an unhealthy cycle of procrastination followed by intense bursts of activity. The gamified elements helped somewhat because they created mini-deadlines that kept me more consistently engaged between major due dates.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I received encouraging feedback, it motivated me to engage more and try harder. But when feedback was critical or when I saw poor scores, I'd sometimes avoid the course for a few days while processing the disappointment. I appreciated detailed feedback that helped me understand what to improve, but the emotional impact of negative results was real.
Q18: The course structure with 19 online modules was honestly overwhelming. Having that much content made it hard to prioritize and know where to focus my limited time. The gamified elements helped break things into manageable pieces, which I appreciated. But the sheer volume meant I was always playing catch-up, which affected my ability to engage deeply with any single topic.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier - they added an element of fun and gave me small wins to celebrate. The discussion forums were well-designed for interaction, which suited my learning style. However, the heavy reliance on text-based materials was harder for me as an auditory learner. More audio content or lecture recordings would have made the course more accessible for someone like me.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat - I was quite active in discussions and logged in frequently, but my assignment completion was below 50%. For me, it was about engagement being easier than production. Participating in discussions or watching videos felt manageable, but sitting down to complete a substantial assignment required uninterrupted time and mental energy I often didn't have. The anxiety around assignments also made them easier to avoid.
Q21: I can see how that happens, especially for students who study effectively offline. If someone downloads materials and studies them thoroughly away from the LMS, their activity would look low but their understanding could be solid. That wasn't quite my experience since my exam scores were low, but I know the disconnect between LMS activity and actual learning is real.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing of engagement, and participation in visible activities like discussions. But they completely miss offline learning, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and the emotional and psychological factors affecting a student. They can't capture whether someone is truly understanding material or just clicking through. They also miss the life context that shapes everything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling connected to the material and the learning community. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks - it's about genuine curiosity and investment in understanding. For me, engagement includes the discussions I have with peers, the moments of insight while listening to lectures, and the motivation to keep going despite challenges. It's both intellectual and emotional.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I genuinely wanted to succeed despite my struggles, or the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing other things. It wouldn't capture my conversations with classmates outside the platform or the personal challenges I was navigating that affected my performance. My resilience and effort don't show up in the data - just the outcomes, which don't tell the whole story of my experience.
